name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٣٥)
```

```english
They say, ‘Be either Jews or Christians, that you may be [rightly] guided.’ Say, ‘No, rather, [we will follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.’ (135)
```

```arabic
قُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (١٣٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘We have faith in Allah and what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and that which Moses and Jesus were given, and that which the prophets were given from their Lord; we make no distinction between any of them and to Him do we submit.’ (136)
```

```arabic
فَإِنْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَآ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا هُمْ فِى شِقَاقٍ ۖ فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٣٧)
```

```english
So if they believe in the like of what you believe in, then they are surely guided; but if they turn away, then [know that] they are only [steeped] in defiance. Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (137)
```

```arabic
صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ صِبْغَةً ۖ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ عَـٰبِدُونَ (١٣٨)
```

```english
‘The baptism of Allah, and who baptizes better than Allah? And Him do we worship.’ (138)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَتُحَآجُّونَنَا فِى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ وَلَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُخْلِصُونَ (١٣٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Will you argue with us concerning Allah, while He is our Lord and your Lord, and to us belong our deeds, and to you belong your deeds, and we worship Him dedicatedly?’ (139)
```

```arabic
أَمْ تَقُولُونَ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطَ كَانُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ قُلْ ءَأَنتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَتَمَ شَهَـٰدَةً عِندَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٠)
```

```english
[Ask them,] ‘Do you say that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes were Jews or Christians?’ Say, ‘Is it you who know better, or Allah?’ And who is a greater wrongdoer than someone who conceals a testimony that is with him from Allah? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (140)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤١)
```

```english
That was a nation that has passed: for it there will be what it has earned, and for you there will be what you have earned, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do. (141)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وقالوا كونوا هوداً أو نصارى تهتدوا** — **And they say, ‘Be Jews or Christians and you will be guided’** (2:135). When the Exalted clarified that the true religion (الدين) followed by the children of Ibrahim—namely Ismail, Ishaq, Yaqub, and his descendants—was Islam (الإسلام), the same religion Ibrahim followed as a monotheist (حنيفاً), He concluded from this that the divisions and sects advocated by the various groups of Jews and Christians were matters invented by their own whims (هوساتهم) and manipulated by their own hands because they were in a state of schism (شقاق). Consequently, they split into religious factions and parties, staining the religion of Allah, the Glorified—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) and the religion of unity (الوحدة)—with the colors of desires, personal interests, and greed. This occurred despite the fact that the religion is one, just as the God (الإله) worshipped through the religion is one; it is the religion of Ibrahim, and to it the Muslims must adhere, abandoning the schism of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب).

It is in the nature of this earthly, worldly life (الحيوة الأرضية الدنيوية) to undergo change and transformation within the very course of its continuity, much like nature itself, which serves as its matter (المادة). This necessitates that national customs, etiquettes, and rituals (الشعائر القومية) change among the various branches and sects of nations. Sometimes, this leads to change and deviation in religious rites, and it may cause that which is not part of the religion to enter it, or that which is part of it to be excluded. Worldly interests and goals may take the place of divine religious goals—which is the ultimate affliction of religion (بلية الدين). At that point, the religion becomes colored by the tint of nationalism (القومية), calling toward a goal other than its original one and disciplining people with a character other than its true character. It does not take long before the reprehensible (المنكر)—that which is not of the religion—becomes recognized as good (معروفاً), defended by people because it accords with their whims and desires, while the truly good (المعروف) becomes reprehensible (منكراً), having no protector to guard it nor any shield to preserve it. The matter eventually leads to what we witness today...

In short, His saying, the Exalted: **وقالوا كونوا هوداً أو نصارى** — **And they say, ‘Be Jews or Christians’** is a summary of a detailed meaning: the Jews said, "Be Jews and you will be guided," and the Christians said, "Be Christians and you will be guided." All of this was due to their sectarianism and schism (شقاق).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل بل ملة إبراهيم حنيفاً وما كان من المشركين** — **Say, ‘Rather [we will follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists’** (2:135). This is a response to their claim. That is: Say, rather we follow the creed (ملة) of Ibrahim as a monotheist (حنيفاً), for it is the single creed followed by all your prophets, from Ibrahim downwards. The proponent of this creed, Ibrahim, was not one of the polytheists (المشركين). Had this creed contained these sectarian divisions—which are the accretions (الضمائم) added by innovators through their disagreements—he would have been a polytheist thereby. For whatever is not from the religion of Allah does not call toward Allah, the Glorified, but toward something else, and that is polytheism (الشرك). Thus, this is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) which contains nothing that is not from Allah, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قولوا آمنا بالله وما أنزل إلينا** — **Say, ‘We have faith in Allah, and what has been sent down to us’** (2:136). After He recounted what the Jews and Christians command regarding following their sects, He mentioned the truth (الحق) that is with Him—and the truth speaks—which is the testimony (الشهادة) of faith (الإيمان) in Allah and faith in what is with the prophets, without making any distinction between them; and this is Islam (الإسلام). He specifically mentioned faith in Allah first and separately from what was sent down to the prophets because faith in Allah is primordial (فطري) — *an innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and does not require the evidence of prophethood (النبوة) or the proof of messengership (الرسالة).

Then the Glorified mentioned what was sent down to us, which is the Quran or the Quranic sciences (المعارف القرآنية), and what was sent down to Ibrahim, Ismail, Ishaq, and Yaqub. Then He mentioned what was given to Musa and Isa, singling them out because the discourse is addressed to the Jews and Christians, and they only claim adherence to these two. Then He mentioned what was given to the prophets from their Lord, so that the testimony might encompass all the prophets, thereby making His subsequent statement consistent: **لا نفرق بين أحد منهم** — **we make no distinction between any of them.**

As for the variation in expression—where He used "sent down" (الإنزال) for what is with us and with Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Yaqub, but used "given" (الإيتاء) — *the act of bestowing* — for what was with Musa, Isa, and the prophets—the reason for this may be that the primary expression is "giving" (الإيتاء), as the Exalted said in Surah al-An'am after mentioning Ibrahim and the prophets before and after him: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** — **They are the ones whom We gave the Book, the judgement and prophethood** (6:89). However, the term "giving" (الإيتاء) is not explicit in denoting revelation (الوحي) and sending down (الإنزال), as the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا لُقْمَانَ الْحِكْمَةَ** — **Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom** (31:12), and He said: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** — **Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, the judgement and prophethood** (45:16). Since both the Jews and Christians considered Ibrahim, Ismail, Ishaq, Yaqub, and the Tribes (الأسباط) to be followers of their respective religions—the Jews claiming them as Jews and the Christians as Christians—and their belief was that the true creed of Christianity or Judaism is what was given to Musa and Isa, had it been said: "and what was given to Ibrahim and Ismail," it would not have been explicit that they were personally recipients of a creed through revelation (الوحي) and sending down (الإنزال). It might have been assumed that what they were "given" was merely what Musa and Isa (peace be upon them) were given, attributed to them by way of following, just as "giving" was attributed to the Children of Israel. Therefore, He specifically used the term "sending down" (الإنزال) for Ibrahim and those mentioned with him. As for the prophets before Ibrahim, the People of the Book have no claim regarding them that would necessitate refuting the potential misunderstanding of the phrase: "and what was given to the prophets."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **والأسباط** — **and the Tribes** (2:136). The Tribes (الأسباط) among the Children of Israel are like the tribes (القبائل) among the children of Ismail. A "tribe" (السبط) is a group of people descending from a single father. They were twelve tribes—nations—each of which traced its lineage back to one of the sons of Yaqub, who were twelve; each of them left behind a nation of people.

If "the Tribes" refers to the nations and peoples, then the attribution of "sending down" to them is because they included prophets from their own tribe. If "the Tribes" refers to specific individuals, then they were prophets to whom revelation was sent down; they were not the brothers of Yusuf, as the latter were not prophets. A similar verse is His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْبَاطِ وَعِيسَىٰ** — **and We revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes, and Jesus** (4:163).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فإن آمنوا بمثل ما آمنتم به فقد اهتدوا** — **So if they believe in the like of what you believe in, then they are certainly guided** (2:137). The use of the word "like" (المثل), while the core meaning is "if they believe in what you believe in," is to sever the root of contention and disputation. For if it were said to them, "Believe in what we believe in," they might have said, as they did: "Rather, we believe in what was sent down to us and we disbelieve in what is beyond it." But when it is said to them, "We have believed in that which contains nothing but the truth, so you believe in that which contains the truth like it," they find no way for sophistry or stubbornness, for what they hold in their hands does not contain the pure essence of truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **في شقاق** — **in schism** (2:137). Schism (الشقاق) refers to hypocrisy (النفاق), contention, quarreling, and division.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فسيكفيكهم الله** — **Allah will suffice you against them** (2:137). This is a promise to the Messenger of Allah of victory over them. He has fulfilled His promise and will complete this blessing for the Muslim nation (الأمة) if He wills. Note that this verse is a parenthetical statement (معترضة) between the preceding and succeeding verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **صبغة الله ومن أحسن من الله صبغة** — **The baptism of Allah, and who is better than Allah in baptism?** (2:138). The word "baptism" (الصبغة) — *literally 'dye' or 'coloring'* — is a noun denoting a type of coloring. That is: this aforementioned faith is a divine coloring for us, and it is the best of colorings, not the coloring of Judaism or Christianity characterized by division in religion and the failure to establish it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ونحن له عابدون** — **and Him do we worship** (2:138). This is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال), serving as an explanation of the reason for His saying: "The baptism of Allah, and who is better than Allah..."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل أتحاجوننا في الله** — **Say, ‘Do you dispute with us concerning Allah’** (2:139). This is a rejection (إنكار) of the People of the Book's disputation with the Muslims concerning Allah, the Glorified. He clarified the reason for this rejection and why their disputation is futile and false by saying: **وهو ربنا وربكم ولنا أعمالنا ولكم أعمالكم ونحن له مخلصون** — **while He is our Lord and your Lord? Our deeds belong to us, and your deeds belong to you, and we are dedicated to Him** (2:139). The explanation is that the disputation of any two groups of followers regarding their followed object (المتبوع) and their quarreling over it only occurs for one of three reasons: either because each group has a different followed object and they wish to argue for the superiority of their own over the other—such as a dispute between an idolater and a Muslim; or because both or one of them seeks a greater exclusivity with the followed object and to invalidate the claim, proximity, or similar status of the other, while the followed object is one; or because one of them possesses characteristics and traits that make it inappropriate for them to be associated with this followed object, or their actions are such that they would cause dishonor or degradation to the followed object. These are the causes of disputation and quarreling between any two groups of followers. However, the Muslims and the People of the Book worship one God, and the deeds of each group do not interfere with the other, and the Muslims are sincere (مخلصون) in their religion to Allah. Thus, there is no reason the People of the Book can cling to in their disputation. Therefore, He first rejected their disputation and then negated each of its three causes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أم تقولون ان ابراهيم** — **Do you say that Abraham...** until His saying: **كانوا هوداً أو نصارى** — **were Jews or Christians** (2:140). This refers to the claim of each of the two groups that Ibrahim and those mentioned after him were from them, which implies they were Jews or Christians, or their explicit claim that they were Jews or Christians, as indicated by the literal meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ فِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمَا أُنزِلَتِ التَّوْرَاةُ وَالْإِنجِيلُ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — **O People of the Book! Why do you dispute concerning Abraham, while the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him? Do you not exercise your reason?** (3:65).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل أأنتم أعلم أم الله** — **Say, ‘Do you know better, or Allah?’** (2:140). For Allah has informed us and informed you in the Book that Musa, Isa, and their two Books came after Ibrahim and those mentioned with him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ومن أظلم ممن كتم شهادة عنده من الله** — **And who is more unjust than him who conceals a testimony he has from Allah?** (2:140). That is, who conceals the testimony he bears that Allah informed him that the legislation of Judaism or Christianity occurred after Ibrahim and those mentioned with him. Thus, the testimony mentioned in the verse is a "testimony of bearing" (شهادة تحمل). Alternatively, the meaning is that he conceals Allah's testimony that these individuals existed before the Torah and the Gospel, in which case the testimony is a "testimony of performance" (شهادة أداء). The first meaning is the one that is determined.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تلك أُمة قد خلت** — **That was a nation that has passed away** (2:141). That is, delving into these individuals and who they were will not benefit your condition, nor will remaining silent about disputing and arguing over them harm you. What is obligatory upon you is to occupy yourselves with what you will be questioned about tomorrow. The repetition of the verse twice is because they were excessive in this disputation which benefits their condition in no way, especially given their knowledge that Ibrahim lived before Judaism and Christianity. Otherwise, researching the state of the prophets and messengers in ways that are beneficial—such as the merits of their messages and the virtues of their noble souls—is something the Quran encourages, as it narrates their stories and commands reflection upon them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قل بل ملة إبراهيم حنيفاً** — **Say, ‘Rather [we will follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif’**, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: "The *Hanifiyyah* (الحنيفية) is in Islam."

And from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "The *Hanifiyyah* left nothing out, to the extent that it includes trimming the mustache, clipping the nails, and circumcision."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: "Allah sent down the *Hanifiyyah* to Ibrahim, and it is purity (الطهارة). It consists of ten things: five for the head and five for the body. As for those for the head, they are: trimming the mustache, letting the beard grow, cutting the hair (طم الشعر), using the tooth-stick (السواك), and using the toothpick (الخلال). As for those for the body, they are: removing body hair, circumcision, clipping the nails, the major ritual bath (الغسل) after sexual ritual impurity (الجنابة), and purification with water. This is the pure *Hanifiyyah* brought by Ibrahim; it has not been abrogated, nor will it be abrogated until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** *Tamm al-sha'r* (طم الشعر) means cutting it or letting it grow thick. Regarding the meaning of this narration, or something close to it, there are very many hadiths narrated by both sects in their books.

In *al-Kafi* and *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قولوا آمنا بالله** — **Say, ‘We have faith in Allah’** (2:136), he said: "He only meant thereby Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn, and it continued after them in the Imams..." (the hadith).

**I say:** This is inferred from the fact that the address occurs at the end of Ibrahim's prayer: **ومن ذريتنا أمة مسلمة لك** — **and from our descendants a nation submissive to You** (2:128). This does not contradict the address being directed to the general Muslims and their being obligated by it, for these addresses have general and specific applications according to the levels of their meaning, as previously discussed regarding Islam, faith (الإيمان), and their levels.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi* from one of the two [Imams], and in *al-Ma'ani* from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **صبغة الله** — **The baptism of Allah** (2:138), he said: "The baptism (الصبغة) is Islam."

**I say:** This is what is apparent from the context of the verses.

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Ma'ani* from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "He colored the believers with the Mastership (الولاية) — *the divine authority and guardianship* — in the Covenant (الميثاق)."

**I say:** This is from the inner meaning (باطن) of the verse, as we shall explain its meaning, and also explain the meaning of *Wilayah* (الولاية) and the meaning of the Covenant (الميثاق), Allah the Mighty willing.
